storm.summary_layperson: Thunderstorm cells are about 27 miles to the southwest, but with very little atmospheric energy available they are expected to weaken on approach — expect a chance of light showers rather than a severe storm.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Sound water at 68°F is only 4°F cooler than the air, producing a weak marine layer that provides minimal shielding compared to a typical summer storm day.
storm.detail_html:
KOKX radar at 20:58 UTC shows three storm cells 27–29 miles to the southwest and WSW, with peak reflectivity of 44 dBZ. No active NWS watches, advisories, or warnings are in effect for Greenwich.

The NWS Area Forecast Discussion notes that the highest instability sits over NE New Jersey and the Lower Hudson Valley — well west of Greenwich — and describes the available CAPE as "tall/skinny, less than 500 J/kg." HRRR confirms: only 30 J/kg of CAPE near the cove at f01, rising to 60 J/kg at f02. These values are too low to sustain vigorous convection. Bulk wind shear is 32–37 kt from the WSW, which could organize storms under higher-CAPE conditions, but with this little instability the shear contributes little additional risk.

The cells are approaching from the SW and will cross Long Island Sound before reaching the cove. With a water–air temperature delta of only 4°F (Sound water 68°F vs. air 72°F), the marine stable layer over the Sound is weak — the pipeline's protective mechanism flag is not active tonight. Some Sound-crossing attenuation is still possible given the SW approach angle and negligible CAPE, but this is a partial effect, not a reliable shield.

The most likely outcome for the cove is a brief period of light rain showers consistent with the NWS forecast ("Chance Rain Showers, 50%"). Surrounding inland areas — where instability is marginally higher — may see slightly more robust activity. A thunderstorm at the cove is possible but unlikely given the near-zero CAPE here.

What would change this call: if radar reflectivity at these cells climbs above 50 dBZ and cells remain organized on approach, upgrade the risk. No upstream APRS gust preview is available — all nearby stations are reporting near-calm, so there is no pre-arrival warning signal yet.

wind.tactics_blurb: Drifter-class conditions through the evening race window — RYC is showing a 2.8 kt ten-minute average with no persistent direction. Keep to the center of the course and respond to individual puffs; no side is reliably favored.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime: B-light (gradient-only, near-calm). The thermal/sea-breeze ingredient is missing: the land–water temperature differential is only 4.0°F (KHPN inland 71.6°F, Sound water 68°F at BRHC3) — below the 5°F gate required for a meaningful thermal driver. The synoptic gradient is also nearly absent: ensemble authoritative mean speed is just 3.8 kt. This places tonight firmly in B-light — wind direction and magnitude are governed by local eddying and boundary-layer noise, not organized flow.

RYC ground truth at 17:10 EDT: NW 308°, 4.6 kt, gusting 5.3 kt, 10-min avg 2.8 kt. That is a real but faint offshore puff. Across the ensemble, direction agreement is only 0.31 — less than one in three stations agrees on direction. Western LIS leans NNW (RYC, Port Chester corridor), central LIS leans SW–SSW (KBDR, KHVN), and eastern LIS shows southerly (Block Island, Westerly). This is a fragmented, near-calm wind field with no coherent regional driver.

The HRRR f01–f06 forecast shows 1–3 kt with wildly cycling directions (NNW → ESE → SE → S → SSE → SSW across the evening hours). At these speeds, HRRR surface wind direction is essentially numerical noise — the model is resolving eddying in a nearly-windless boundary layer, not a real wind regime. Do not trade on individual HRRR direction calls.

Sunset tonight is approximately 20:20 EDT. In B-light there is no thermal collapse (there was no thermal to begin with), but the already-faint gradient may soften slightly after dark as the nocturnal boundary layer stabilizes. HRRR shows speeds dropping toward 1 kt after 20:00.

Cove geography note: with no established wind direction, the pressure-by-side table yields no reliable call. Center of the course sees the most consistent air, away from immediate shoreline sheltering. Stay center, react to individual puffs, do not commit to a side. One wildcard: if the SW storm cells (27 mi out) produce outflow as they approach, brief gusty W–NW puffs are possible in the 18:00–20:00 window — these could temporarily upgrade conditions to 8–12 kt before returning to calm.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 observation is 129 minutes old — wind and SST data may be stale
HRRR f10–f12 not available for this cycle
Ensemble direction agreement very low (0.31) — wind field is fragmented and near-calm; directional guidance is approximate
